reason: The item cleanly tests whether a linear advancement-in-time trend and charge/discharge heat asymmetry justify a “predictable and proportionally manageable” risk claim, separating trend predictability from absolute safety margin and mechanistic inference. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, pushing experts to respect temperature-threshold sequencing (SEI vs cathode/electrolyte) and avoid mechanism-overclaims from macroscopic linearity. Discrimination is strong but not S-tier because some options lean on unstated numerics (A) and one decoy (D) risks subjective interpretation of “most dangerous regime/thermal margin.”

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware deployment judgment: it forces readers to separate experimental metrics (recall/F1, time-step sensitivity) from system-level guarantees and to reason about distribution shift and sampling-rate changes. Decoys are largely plausible and probe common overclaims (latency-to-intervention, thermal-only sensing replacing electrochemical monitoring), though one incorrect option can be rejected on multiple grounds, slightly reducing single-fault purity and discriminative sharpness.

The prompt cleanly targets a single boundary-aware judgment: why two near-overlapping energy-fraction findings cannot justify an impossibility claim without worst-case transfer and condition dependence. It strongly rewards experts who recognize distribution-vs-bound confusion and threshold non-universality, while requiring specific missing-variable identification (geometry/spacing, SOC, trigger conditions). The main quality risk is that “less than 10% available to interact with neighboring cells” could be interpreted as either a hard cap or an average, which may allow multiple defensible phrasings of the error and slightly reduces decoy/diagnostic sharpness.
